steps.title: Identify the coordinates and use perpendicular bisector geometry
steps.content:
From the graph, the three points are at (8, 7), (11, 10), and (11, 4). Observe that two points, (11, 10) and (11, 4), share the same x-coordinate, forming a vertical chord. A key fact: the center of a circle lies on the perpendicular bisector of any chord. For a vertical chord, this perpendicular bisector is a horizontal line passing through the chord's midpoint.

The midpoint of the chord is at:
$$\left(11, \frac{10 + 4}{2}\right) = (11, 7)$$
So the center lies on the horizontal line $y = 7$, meaning the center is at $(h, 7)$ for some value $h$ to be determined.

steps.title: Solve for h using the equidistance property
steps.content:
The center $(h, 7)$ is equidistant from all three points. Notice that (8, 7) also has y-coordinate 7, so its distance from the center is purely horizontal: $|h - 8|$. This distance must equal the distance from $(h, 7)$ to $(11, 10)$:
$$|h - 8| = \sqrt{(h - 11)^2 + (7 - 10)^2} = \sqrt{(h - 11)^2 + 9}$$
Squaring both sides (noting that $h > 8$):
$$(h - 8)^2 = (h - 11)^2 + 9$$
Expanding both sides:
$$h^2 - 16h + 64 = h^2 - 22h + 121 + 9$$
The $h^2$ terms cancel:
$$-16h + 64 = -22h + 130$$
Solving for $h$:
$$6h = 66 \implies h = 11$$

steps.title: Calculate the circumference
steps.content:
The center is at (11, 7). The radius is the distance from the center to the point (8, 7):
$$r = 11 - 8 = 3$$
The circumference is:
$$C = 2\pi r = 2\pi(3) = 6\pi$$
Since $C = k\pi$, we have $k = 6$. That matches choice B — B is correct.
